title: Security: 13 known CVEs in examples/celery/requirements.txt
body:
## Summary

The `examples/celery/requirements.txt` file contains pinned dependency versions with **13 known security vulnerabilities** (1 HIGH, 11 MEDIUM, 1 LOW), identified via Trivy filesystem scan.

## Affected File

`examples/celery/requirements.txt` — pip-compiled lockfile for the Celery example application.

## Vulnerabilities

### HIGH (1)

| Package | CVE | Installed | Fixed | Description |
|---------|-----|-----------|-------|-------------|
| werkzeug | [CVE-2024-34069](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2024-34069) | 2.3.3 | 3.0.3 | Code execution on developer's machine via debugger |

### MEDIUM (11)

| Package | CVE | Installed | Fixed | Description |
|---------|-----|-----------|-------|-------------|
| jinja2 | [CVE-2024-22195](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2024-22195) | 3.1.2 | 3.1.3 | HTML attribute injection via xmlattr |
| jinja2 | [CVE-2024-34064](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2024-34064) | 3.1.2 | 3.1.4 | Non-attribute character acceptance in keys |
| jinja2 | [CVE-2024-56201](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2024-56201) | 3.1.2 | 3.1.5 | Sandbox breakout through malicious filenames |
| jinja2 | [CVE-2024-56326](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2024-56326) | 3.1.2 | 3.1.5 | Sandbox breakout through indirect reference to format method |
| jinja2 | [CVE-2025-27516](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2025-27516) | 3.1.2 | 3.1.6 | Sandbox breakout through attr filter selecting format method |
| werkzeug | [CVE-2023-46136](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2023-46136) | 2.3.3 | 2.3.8 | High resource consumption (DoS) |
| werkzeug | [CVE-2024-49766](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2024-49766) | 2.3.3 | 3.0.6 | safe_join not safe on Windows |
| werkzeug | [CVE-2024-49767](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2024-49767) | 2.3.3 | 3.0.6 | Resource exhaustion when parsing file data in forms |
| werkzeug | [CVE-2025-66221](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2025-66221) | 2.3.3 | 3.1.4 | DoS via Windows device names in path segments |
| werkzeug | [CVE-2026-21860](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2026-21860) | 2.3.3 | 3.1.5 | safe_join() allows Windows special device names with compound extensions |
| werkzeug | [CVE-2026-27199](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2026-27199) | 2.3.3 | 3.1.6 | safe_join() allows Windows special device names |

### LOW (1)

| Package | CVE | Installed | Fixed | Description |
|---------|-----|-----------|-------|-------------|
| flask | [CVE-2026-27205](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2026-27205) | 2.3.2 | 3.1.3 | Information disclosure via improper caching of session data |

## Previous Attempts

- PR #5987 attempted to fix only CVE-2024-34069 (werkzeug) but was closed without merge (2026-04-09). This issue covers all 13 CVEs comprehensively.

## Proposed Fix

Update the pinned versions in `examples/celery/requirements.txt`:
- `flask` 2.3.2 → 3.1.3
- `werkzeug` 2.3.3 → 3.1.6
- `jinja2` 3.1.2 → 3.1.6
- `blinker` 1.6.2 → 1.9.0 (flask 3.x dependency)
- `itsdangerous` 2.1.2 → 2.2.0 (flask 3.x dependency)
- `markupsafe` 2.1.2 → 3.0.3 (compatible upgrade)

## Verification

Trivy rescan after fix shows **0 vulnerabilities** across all severity levels.

## Scanner

Tool: [Trivy](https://github.com/aquasecurity/trivy) v0.57.0, DB updated 2026-04-10

title: Docs: doubled word "the the" in TESTING config description
body:
In `docs/config.rst`, the description for `TESTING` contains a doubled word:

```
Exceptions are propagated rather than handled by the
the app's error handlers.
```

Should be:

```
Exceptions are propagated rather than handled by the app's error handlers.
```

**File:** `docs/config.rst` line 83

title: fix: celery task result endpoint crashes on task failure
body:
## Description

When a Celery task fails, `result.ready()` evaluates to `True` but `result.successful()` is `False`. 
Calling `result.get()` without `propagate=False` on a failed task will re-raise the task's exception, causing the Flask endpoint to crash with a 500 Internal Server Error instead of returning the task's failed status. 
Additionally, if `result.result` is an Exception object, returning it directly in the dictionary will cause a `TypeError` during JSON serialization.


**Severity**: `medium`
**File**: `examples/celery/src/task_app/views.py`

## Expected Behavior

The code should handle this case properly to avoid unexpected errors or degraded quality.

title: Flask 3.1.3 test (`test_environ_for_valid_idna_completes`) breaks after Werkzeug update from 3.1.6 to 3.1.7
body:
Flask 3.1.3 test (`test_environ_for_valid_idna_completes`) breaks after Werkzeug update from 3.1.6 to 3.1.7.

To replicate the bug you just need to run testsuite:
```
$ python3 -m pytest -vra

<...>

=================================== FAILURES ===================================
____________________ test_environ_for_valid_idna_completes _____________________

    def test_environ_for_valid_idna_completes():
        app = flask.Flask(__name__)

        @app.route("/")
        def index():
            return "Hello World!"

        from flask.testing import EnvironBuilder

        builder = EnvironBuilder(app)
        environ = builder.get_environ()

        # these characters are all IDNA-compatible
        environ["HTTP_HOST"] = "ąśźäüжŠßя.com"

        with app.request_context(environ):
            response = app.full_dispatch_request()

>       assert response.status_code == 200
E       assert 400 == 200
E        +  where 400 = <Response streamed [400 BAD REQUEST]>.status_code

tests/test_reqctx.py:314: AssertionError
=========================== short test summary info ============================
FAILED tests/test_reqctx.py::test_environ_for_valid_idna_completes - assert 4...
```

Environment:

- Python version: 3.13.12
- Flask version: 3.1.3
- Werkzeug version: 3.1.7

title: docs: Missing Redis setup instructions in Celery example README
body:
## Description

The `examples/celery/README.md` does not mention that **Redis is required** 
as a message broker for Celery to work. A beginner following the instructions 
will successfully start the Celery worker but immediately see repeated 
connection errors like:
```
ERROR/MainProcess] consumer: Cannot connect to redis://localhost:6379//: 
Error 111 connecting to localhost:6379. Connection refused..
Trying again in 2.00 seconds... (1/100)
```

This is confusing because nothing in the README warns about this dependency.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Follow the README instructions exactly
2. Run `celery -A make_celery worker --loglevel INFO`
3. Celery starts but immediately fails trying to connect to Redis
4. Nothing in the README explains why or how to fix it

## Expected Behavior

The README should mention that Redis is required and provide 
setup instructions for it.

## Proposed Fix

Add the following before the `celery` run command in `README.md`:
```rst
Celery requires a message broker. Install and start Redis before 
running the worker:

$ sudo apt install redis-server   # Debian/Ubuntu
$ sudo service redis start

# Verify Redis is running:
$ redis-cli ping
# Expected output: PONG
```

## Environment

- OS: Ubuntu / Debian (Linux)
- Flask version: latest (main branch)
- Celery version: 5.6.2

## Additional Notes

This is a documentation-only change with no impact on code. 
It improves the onboarding experience for beginners on 
Debian/Ubuntu systems, which are the most common Linux distributions.

title: flask run should support pep723
body:
Because no one wants to use:

native pkgutil-style, declarative, non-declarative, setup.py, setup.cfg, pyproject.toml, pep517, setuptools, build, pip, namespace packages,  normal packages, pkg-util namespace package

You name it because I won't.

Having `flask run` or any such primitive commands to allow running a flask application would make the entire process go from a 2 weeks pip marathon to a 10 minutes max of relaxing time where it just works magically.

title: Clarify recommended debug workflow in Quickstart documentation
body:
While reviewing the Quickstart documentation (https://flask.palletsprojects.com/en/stable/quickstart/#debug-mode), I noticed that debug mode is discussed but the distinction between using `app.run(debug=True)` and the recommended `flask run --debug` CLI flag is not clearly emphasized.

Many external tutorials still reference `app.run(debug=True)`, and new users may not understand when to use the CLI flag versus enabling debug mode directly in application code.

It may be helpful to clarify:

- That `flask run --debug` is the recommended development workflow
- When `app.run(debug=True)` is appropriate
- Why separating debug configuration from application code is considered best practice

title: [Security] Default session HMAC digest should be SHA-256, not SHA-1
body:
## Summary

Flask's `SecureCookieSessionInterface` uses SHA-1 as the default HMAC digest for signing session cookies. SHA-1 has been deprecated by NIST (since 2011) and is no longer recommended for cryptographic use.

## Location

`src/flask/sessions.py`, line 293:

```python
digest_method = staticmethod(_lazy_sha1)

title: Add a built-in is_safe_redirect() helper to handle browser URL normalization (backslash → forward slash)
body:
Modern browsers normalize backslashes in navigation URLs (e.g., `\\evil.com`) into forward slashes (`//evil.com`). This creates a discrepancy between how Flask/Werkzeug evaluates redirect targets and how user agents ultimately interpret them.

A common pattern used by Flask developers to prevent protocol-relative redirects is:

```python
if not url.startswith("//"):
    return redirect(url)
```

However, the following payload bypasses this logic:

```
\\evil.com
```

Flask/Werkzeug evaluates this as a safe relative path (`startswith("//") == False`), while browsers normalize it to:

```
https://evil.com
```

and perform an external redirect. This normalization behavior is consistent across Chrome, Firefox, and Safari.

As Flask does not provide a built-in redirect validation helper, developers routinely implement ad-hoc string checks that do not account for browser normalization rules, resulting in an ecosystem-wide class of open-redirect flaws.

**Feature Request**

Introduce an official, browser-aware redirect safety utility in Flask or Werkzeug, similar to Django’s `is_safe_url`, to standardize redirect validation across the framework. The helper should:

1. Normalize backslashes to forward slashes before validation.
2. Reject protocol-relative URLs (`//example.com`).
3. Optionally enforce allowed host checks.
4. Provide a documented, recommended pattern for safe redirects.

**Rationale**

- This issue is not a flaw in Flask’s redirect implementation but a recurring security footgun for developers due to inconsistent handling between application logic and browser URL normalization.
- Providing an official helper reduces the likelihood of open-redirect vulnerabilities in applications built on Flask.
- This is framework hardening rather than a security bug report, and it aligns with the expectations set by other frameworks that offer standardized redirect-safety utilities.

This feature would give developers a reliable and recommended approach to validating redirect targets securely, reducing reliance on fragile custom logic.

title: Add configurable JSON parsing limits and preprocessing hook
body:
Description
Flask currently parses JSON input using json.loads() without providing an application-level mechanism to enforce payload size limits or preprocess raw JSON before deserialization.
This feature would introduce configuration settings such as:
•⁠  ⁠JSON_MAX_STRING_LENGTH – to reject JSON payloads exceeding a defined size before parsing.
•⁠  ⁠JSON_PREPROCESS_RAW – to allow an optional callable to preprocess raw JSON text before json.loads() is executed.

Example of the Problem This Would Resolve
If a client sends a very large JSON payload (for example, 15–20MB), Flask will attempt to parse it entirely in memory. This can lead to unnecessary memory consumption and CPU usage, especially in API-heavy environments. While infrastructure-level limits (e.g., reverse proxies) may mitigate this, application-level safeguards provide defense-in-depth and improve reliability.
Currently, this problem can only be addressed by subclassing JSONProvider, overriding loads(), or modifying framework internals. Providing built-in configuration support would make it easier to enforce consistent JSON parsing policies without custom framework patches.

title: Built-in /docs endpoint similar to FastAPI for automatic API documentation
body:
## Feature Request

It would be extremely helpful if Express provided a built-in `/docs` endpoint (similar to FastAPI) that automatically generates interactive API documentation.

## Motivation

Frameworks like FastAPI provide automatic OpenAPI/Swagger documentation at `/docs` by default. This significantly improves:

- Developer experience
- Faster API testing
- Quick onboarding for contributors
- Reduced dependency on external tools like Postman for small APIs

Currently in Express, developers need to manually integrate third-party packages such as:
- swagger-ui-express
- swagger-jsdoc
- Redoc

While these solutions work well, having an optional built-in documentation middleware would make Express even more developer-friendly, especially for small projects, prototypes, and teaching environments.

## Proposed Solution

Provide an optional built-in middleware that:

- Auto-detects registered routes
- Generates OpenAPI schema automatically
- Serves interactive documentation at `/docs`
- Can be enabled with something like:

```js
app.enableDocs();

title: Report: publish security analysis findings and recommendations
body:
Phase: Report

Tasks:
- Synthesize mapping, quality, and problems into a concise report
- Post report to main issue as a comment
- Close all phase sub-issues and mark main issue done

Will close this sub-issue when the report is published and other sub-issues closed.

title: Problems: identify security issues and risky patterns
body:
Phase: Problems

Tasks:
- From mapping and quality findings, list concrete security problems (misconfigurations, risky APIs, missing docs)
- Prioritize by severity and exploitability
- Create actionable recommendations for maintainers

Will close this sub-issue when problems are documented.

title: Quality: review security-related files and code
body:
Phase: Quality

Tasks:
- Locate SECURITY.md or similar docs
- Review core source files for security patterns (req handling, parameter parsing, header handling)
- Search codebase for uses of 'eval', 'new Function', 'child_process', 'crypto' misuse, and unsafe defaults
- Document findings and examples

Will close this sub-issue when analysis is complete.

title: Mapping: repository tree, branches, commits
body:
Phase: Mapping

Tasks:
- Retrieve the full repository tree
- List branches
- List recent commits (perPage: 50)
- Attach findings to the main issue as comments

Will close this sub-issue when mapping is complete.

title: 🔍 Security Practices Analysis: expressjs/express
body:
Initiating an automated security practices analysis for the expressjs/express repository.

Planned phases:
1. Mapping: repository tree, branches, commit history
2. Quality: review security-related files (e.g., SECURITY.md, docs, core code), search for security patterns
3. Problems: identify potential security issues and risky patterns
4. Report: summarize findings and recommendations

Sub-issues will be created for each phase and closed as they complete.

title: [Docs] Sidebar is hidden in mobile layout
body:
## Problem Description

Theoretically, for documentation using the [alabaster](https://github.com/sphinx-doc/alabaster) theme, the sidebar content should appear at either the **very top** or the **very bottom** in a mobile layout. For example:

- https://alabaster.readthedocs.io/
- https://docs.kernel.org/
- https://www.writethedocs.org/

However, I noticed that the current Requests documentation fails to display sidebar content in its mobile layout.

Based on a preliminary investigation, this issue seems to be caused by modifications to `sphinxsidebar` within the current `custom.css`:

https://github.com/psf/requests/blob/4b0b1a3e9f2fc21b9dcd8b906f2ff02645aa697e/docs/_static/custom.css#L9-L13

The simplest fix is to delete the following line:

```
  div.sphinxsidebar {display: none;}
```

title: [Docs] No clickable button in other pages can return back to the Home page
body: ## Problem Description When visiting the [Quickstart](https://requests.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user/quickstart/) page of the Requests documentation, there is currently no clickable button to return to the [Home](https://requests.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) page. This could be resolved if other pages (including Quickstart) featured a logo at the top of the left sidebar that links back to the home page, consistent with the Home page itself. The cause of this behavior seems to be as follows: First, in the `html_sidebars` configuration within `conf.py`, the top sidebar template is set to `sidebarintro.html` only for the `index` page. For all other pages, the top template is set to `sidebarlogo.html`: https://github.com/psf/requests/blob/ef439eb779c1eba7cbdeeeb302b11e1e061b4b7d/docs/conf.py#L174-L184 Currently, the hyperlinked logo is only implemented in `sidebarintro.html`: https://github.com/psf/requests/blob/ef439eb779c1eba7cbdeeeb302b11e1e061b4b7d/docs/_templates/sidebarintro.html#L1-L5

title: [Docs] Content in left sidebar cannot be extracted by gettext builder
body: ## Problem Description The current content in the left sidebar of the Requests documentation: is generated from `sidebarlogo.html` or `sidebarintro.html`: https://github.com/psf/requests/blob/ef439eb779c1eba7cbdeeeb302b11e1e061b4b7d/docs/_templates/sidebarintro.html#L12-L34 https://github.com/psf/requests/blob/ef439eb779c1eba7cbdeeeb302b11e1e061b4b7d/docs/_templates/sidebarlogo.html#L6-L29 While the `sphinx-build -b gettext` command can extract translatable strings from `.rst` or `.md` files, strings within Jinja templates cannot be extracted unless it is wrapped in `{{ _('XXX') }}` or `{% trans %}XXX{% endtrans %}`.

title: `check_header_validity` does not reject null bytes and ASCII control characters in header names and values
body:
**Summary:**
`check_header_validity` in `requests/utils.py` silently accepts null bytes (`\x00`) and other ASCII control characters (`\x01`–`\x1f`, `\x7f`) in both header names and values. RFC 7230 Section 3.2.6 forbids these characters in header fields. Only `\r` and `\n` are currently caught.


**Expected Result:**
`check_header_validity` should raise `InvalidHeader` for any header name or value containing null bytes or ASCII control characters, consistent with how `\r` and `\n` are already handled.


**Actual Result:**
```python
from requests.utils import check_header_validity
check_header_validity(('X-Test', 'val\x00ue'))   # no error raised
check_header_validity(('X-Test', 'val\x01ue'))   # no error raised
check_header_validity(('X-Test\x00', 'value'))   # no error raised
```


**Reproduction Steps:**
```python
from requests.utils import check_header_validity
from requests.exceptions import InvalidHeader

# All of these should raise InvalidHeader but do not:
check_header_validity(('X-Test', 'val\x00ue'))
check_header_validity(('X-Test', '\x01value'))
check_header_validity(('X-Test', 'value\x7f'))
check_header_validity(('X-Test\x00', 'value'))

# These correctly raise InvalidHeader:
# check_header_validity(('X-Test', 'val\nue'))   # blocked
# check_header_validity(('X-Test', 'val\rue'))   # blocked
```


**System Information:**
```bash
$ python -m requests.help
```

title: Clarify problematic chardet dependency warning
body:
## How to reproduce

Install requests and chardet in a clean environment:

```bash
mkdir test && cd test && uv init
uv add requests chardet
uv run python -c "import requests"
```

At the time of writing (requests=2.32.5, chardet=7.2.0), this gives a warning which is hard to understand, and is not actionable:

> RequestsDependencyWarning: urllib3 (2.6.3) or chardet (7.2.0)/charset_normalizer (3.4.6) doesn't match a supported version!


## Explanation

I spent around 15 minutes trying to get rid of this warning, thinking that maybe my network requests would somehow be broken. I tried to install different versions of urllib3, chardet and charset-normalizer. After reading issues and docs, I found the full explanation to be: requests uses `chardet` only if it's installed (optional dependency). Otherwise it uses `charset-normalizer`. I think this behavior is questionable - consider that I didn't even install chardet myself, one of my other dependencies had chardet as its dependency. Why would that alter the behavior of requests?

Furthermore, I had to go into requests code to understand what chardet version was actually supported, which is not a very good developer experience.

## Suggestion

If existing behavior is kept, I suggest to at least reword the warning, e.g.:
> The requests library uses chardet as an optional dependency. You have chardet==7.2.0, but requests supports chardet<6. Please install chardet<6 to silence this warning.

title: check_compatibility() naively expects only released versions of chardet and charset_normalizer
body:
Having chardet 6.0.0dev0 installed in a virtual environment results in the compatibly check failing due to ValueError because 0dev0 is not a valid integer.

## Expected Result

The compatibly check passed.

## Actual Result

```
/tmp/tmp.y15VRk8O9t/lib/python3.12/site-packages/requests/__init__.py:113: RequestsDependencyWarning: urllib3 (2.6.3) or chardet (6.0.0dev0)/charset_normalizer (3.4.6) doesn't match a supported version!

```

Starting with an empty virtualenv:

  },
  "requests": {
    "version": "2.32.5"
  },
  "system_ssl": {
    "version": "300000d0"
  },
  "urllib3": {
    "version": "2.6.3"
  },
  "using_charset_normalizer": false,
  "using_pyopenssl": false
}
```

<!-- This command is only available on Requests v2.16.4 and greater. Otherwise,
please provide some basic information about your system (Python version,
operating system, &c). -->

title: use milestones for better release management
body:
I noticed that the milestones here need some cleanup, some seem "completed", but are not closed (2.32.0, Bankruptcy(?)).

Also, a milestone for next patch release (2.32.6) is missing and that would be especially interesting considering that quite a few people are waiting for it due to the chardet warning issue (#7219).

If it would be obvious what is still to do, it would be easier to help.

title: Follow redirects - timeout waiting for connection to close
body:
<!-- Summary. -->
When requests sees a redirect header, it waits until the connection has closed before following it. This can cause timeout errors.

## Expected Result

The redirect should be followed immediately.
<!-- What you expected. -->

## Actual Result
Requests waits for the body before following, which may cause it to timeout.
<!-- What happened instead. -->

## Reproduction Steps
Consider this server:
```php
<?php
header( "Location: https://example.com/" );
//   Long operation.
sleep(10);
die();
```

That immediately sends:

```
< HTTP/2 302 
< location: https://example.com/
```

But then waits before closing the connection.

A simple Python request:

```python
import requests
requests.get("https://example.com/test.php", stream=True, allow_redirects=True)
```

Waits for 10 seconds before following the redirect.  This happens if stream is False, allow_redirects is False etc.

Running `curl -L https://example.com/test.php` ignores the body and starts following immediately:

```
< HTTP/2 302 
< location: https://example.com/
* Ignoring the response-body
* Issue another request to this URL: 'https://example.com/'
```

## System Information

    $ python -m requests.help

```json
{
  "chardet": {
    "version": "3.0.4"
  },
  "charset_normalizer": {
    "version": "2.0.12"
  },
  "cryptography": {
    "version": "3.2.1"
  },
  "idna": {
    "version": ""
  },
  "implementation": {
    "name": "CPython",
    "version": "3.6.8"
  },
  "platform": {
    "release": "4.18.0-553.54.1.lve.el8.x86_64",
    "system": "Linux"
  },
  "pyOpenSSL": {
    "openssl_version": "101010bf",
    "version": "19.0.0"
  },
  "requests": {
    "version": "2.27.1"
  },
  "system_ssl": {
    "version": "101010bf"
  },
  "urllib3": {
    "version": "1.24.2"
  },
  "using_charset_normalizer": false,
  "using_pyopenssl": true
}
```

<!-- This command is only available on Requests v2.16.4 and greater. Otherwise,
please provide some basic information about your system (Python version,
operating system, &c). -->

## Additional

I am aware that this is a stupid problem and my server should be terminating the connection quickly!

title: New chardet version
body:
I apologise in advance for what may be a complete non-issue. I do not have the skills or the time to comb through the chardet codebase for potential backdoors.

The chardet project has recently released a new version(7) that was "rewritten from the ground up". Although most of the buzz around this is about the license changing from LGPL to MIT, I am concerned that a trusted component of practically every python internet-connected application has been swapped out for a completely new codebase. And it seems requests has accepted this by bumping the chardet version requirement to < 8.0.

If someone wanted to compromise every python app in the world, backdooring chardet would seem to be a great solution, given it processes every piece of user data. Making an incremental change to do this would risk easy discovery, so claiming a complete rewrite makes verification a massively harder (and therefore more unlikely) job.

I really hope someone smarter than me can explain why this could not happen. Otherwise it would seem prudent to move the chardet version requirement back to < 7.0 until chardet has been reliably vetted.
